data.title: Short Selling And Put Buying Point To Big Tech Rally
data.description:
Huge amounts of put buying and short selling in the technology sector point to higher prices in this sector. We wrote about this last December when this activity first began, but it’s even more so today (technology stocks appear ready for another six-month advance).

This conclusion contradicts the forecast of the imminent popping of the tech bubble, which so many writers and analysts have made. Let me show you.

Sentiment is Better at Forecasting Markets Than Valuations

History has shown that valuation ratios are terrible at forecasting the market. Investors appear to be making that mistake again. Prices can stay overvalued for years before correcting.

A far better tool is measuring investor sentiment or expectations. That’s what we specialize in. And right now our tools are saying that there are far too many investors who think this market is going down. The current levels of short selling and put buying are at levels that normally precede a strong rally.

Let's first take a look at short selling.

Short Selling Indicator

This explanation about short selling indicators is taken from last December’s Article:

“From 1940 until 1985, short selling indicators were the primary tool to measure bearish investor expectations. It worked as a contrary opinion indicator. Large amounts of short selling occurred at price lows while little occurred near the top. This was the opposite of what you would want.

Then by 1985, index arbitrage short selling overwhelmed the calculations, making short selling indicators useless.

This changed around 2010 when ProShares created inverse ETF's that both shorted and leveraged the market. These allowed us once again to measure investor expectations using short selling. We found that investor activity in these funds worked in the same contrary way: investors usually bought short funds in high numbers right before prices were about to rise. “

You can see this in this chart. It shows that the purchasing of the ProShare 2X QQQ short fund is the highest in history. It means that record numbers of investors are expecting the technology sector, which is the primary component of the QQQ, to decline.

Corresponding "put buying" in technology stocks confirms this.

Put Buying in the Technology Sector Confirms This

This last chart shows total directional put buying in all 661 technology stocks. Directional put buying in all 661 surged above April levels and hit all-time highs. It shows that on average over 1.4 billion dollars a day are buying puts expecting a price decline in technology stocks.

Conclusion

Our conclusion is the same as the December article.

“These indicators suggest another advancing wave which could be just as powerful as the April to November wave. These indicators also support the idea that the technology sector will again be its leader as it was in the 1st wave.

This is not a short term outlook. We think a second wave will carry into the summer of next year with the technology sector possibly rising another 60% or 70%.

We must reserve for our subscribers "when" we think this second wave will begin.”

data.title: GDXY: Extreme Distributions, And A Tail Risk Not To Forget
data.description:
When I think about a yield-generating solution on gold, I often think of the YieldMax™ Gold Miners Option Income Strategy ETF (GDXY). This is because today, with gold pushed higher by technical and macro drivers and still pronounced implied volatility, covered call solutions could, in my opinion, have room to stabilize in terms of price and distribute distinctive income. So why hold? Well… here too “not all that glitters is gold”: in the sense that although on a theoretical level everything adds up, in practical terms there is a risk that must be confronted. But before analyzing it…

What is GDXY

With an AUM close to $300M, GDXY is an actively managed ETF that was created with the primary objective of generating current income and the secondary objective of maintaining an indirect exposure to the price of GDX (VanEck Gold Miners ETF). It does this through management based on Synthetic covered call and Synthetic covered call spread (alternatively). All with a total expense ratio of 1.08% and a Median 30-Day Spread of 0.06%

Strategy and Composition

The base consists of synthetic long exposure to GDX through long ATM calls and short ATM puts that replicate 100% of GDX movements. At the same time, the sale of calls with strikes typically 0–15% OTM and maturities ≤ 1 month. All using U.S. Treasuries as collateral + source of income.

From the holding distribution file, this exposure clearly emerges.

Result

First of all, there is a reduced MKT vs NAV spread; therefore, it means that pricing is relatively efficient and there are no macroscopic market distortions. Then, this distribution brings the classic consequences of buy-write:

  • Full exposure to GDX drawdowns
  • Mechanically limited upside

  • A distribution that will depend on GDX implied volatility and on the skew structure.

Volatility

In this regard, what is extremely interesting and, for me, decisive in the analysis is the chart (kurtosis + SKEW) shared in the gold outlook for 2026 by the World Gold Council. It communicates two key elements:

  • Increasing kurtosis: return distribution more “fat” in the tails. Typically this means more frequent extreme movements.
  • Structurally elevated SKEW: market willing to pay more for downside protection.

The positive side from a first reading is, in my opinion, one: the market is willing to pay more to hedge, and therefore option premiums (calls and especially puts) increase. This element can therefore support the distribution at the levels defined by the distribution rate (and also explains the reason why today they are so high). On the negative side, let’s not forget the cap on the increase in the price of GDX.

Expectations on GDX

I recently talked about it in this research: I have a positive view on gold for 2026. A conclusion I reach based on 2 drivers:

  1. Expected average demand for 2026 of ~585 tonnes/quarter shared by JPM
  2. Paradoxically favorable macro growth, fueled by declining rates and a weak dollar

Even though I also share a concern: that at the beginning of 2026 the market has already anticipated part of the expected technical growth (the expected demand) in prices. Not by chance, the first target ($5,000/oz) shared by JPM has already been reached, even if it was expected for Q3 2026.

Risk

The analysis of kurtosis and skew highlights greater exposure to extreme risks. In essence, events once considered rare are becoming less exceptional and increasingly more likely.

Considering that GDX and GDXY practically present the same risk profile in bearish markets, it means that potential losses are increasing at an increasingly rapid pace. The concrete risk for GDXY is that the declines of the underlying are so deep as to weigh more than the increase in the premiums collected.

Therefore, in the event of a negative shock, the gap between the premium collected and the added risk (in capital terms) could widen. And it is true that potentially distributions would remain competitive, but the future ability to generate income weakens precisely because of the price collapse and the cap on potential future upside. This would force (in theory and in my honest opinion) to reinvest capital (or the premiums collected) to avoid the deterioration of the distributive capacity of your investment, and it reduces the concrete gain (what an investor actually puts in their pocket), leaving a bit of an “illusion.” And pay attention; this is a risk that anyone should remember when working with buy-write strategies.

My Opinion

I think that the increase in the probability of a tail risk makes an initial entry at these levels quite risky. To minimize the weight of reinvestment and maintain competitive distributions, personally I prefer to average positions in zones below the 40-50 RSI points.

Conclusion

Therefore, I think that the correct rating is HOLD. On GDX, expectations remain broadly positive; potentially part of the growth is already priced in. Kurtosis + SKEW support premiums; therefore, GDXY could continue to maintain positive price stability and a competitive distribution. At the same time, the probability of tail risk increases, which discourages a BUY rating.

data.title: Axcellis Technologies: I'm Not Too Confident In A Recovery
data.description:
Introduction

Axcelis Technologies, Inc. (ACLS) is about to report its Q4 numbers on February 17th, so I thought it would be a great time to give some updates from me regarding what I think about the company’s performance and future potential. It’s been over a year since I gave it a Hold rating and said I was in no rush to add. During that time, I sold my position for a small loss, as I had better use of the capital since then, and I’m glad I did, as the company’s shares underperformed significantly. My stance remains due to deteriorating profitability and no turnaround in sight for now.

Briefly on Performance

Over the past two years or so, the company’s top-line growth has not been there. We can see that as the company hit 2024, it all started to go downhill. The company got hit particularly hard by cyclicality as customers began to digest the inventory post-2023 buildouts. This was particularly evident in customers in China. Throughout 2024, the demand for the company’s products was soft. Booking fell short, and the auto/industrial segments continue to show weakness.

2025 has been more of the same, it looks like, with massive y/y declines in sales due to continual China digestion with mature/power capacity overhang post-2024 buildout. The slowdown in China’s EV growth has been quite a negative development for many companies involved in the space, including ACLS. It seems that there is still no bottom in the near future, but we will have to keep an eye out.

What about the company’s profitability during these trying times? Unfortunately, a bit of the same here, too. As soon as 2024 started with weakening demand, the company’s margins started to decline as well, with a bright spot being that the company’s gross margins remained relatively strong during this downturn, losing around 40% of revenues. The company enjoyed a favorable CS&I mix, high-margin spares, and warranty/install efficiencies, which offset lower volumes. Operating margins have fallen due to the company losing OpEx leverage as it was not able to distribute fixed OpEx as efficiently as before due to lower revenues.

With the noticeable decline in the company’s bottom line, it’s not surprising to see that other key profitability and efficiency metrics followed suit, specifically ACLS’s ROA, ROE, and ROTC. The management has not been able to utilize the company’s assets and shareholder capital as efficiently as before, and it looks like this is going to continue for a while longer, unless we see some good developments in 2026. The company is losing its pricing power and the strong moat it had in such a niche market. At least that is what ROTC tells me.

Let’s take a look at the company’s financial situation. As of Q3 ’25, ACLS had around $187m in cash and equivalents, as well as $262m in ST investments and no long-term debt, besides some lease obligations. This position is a saving grace for the company because even if this down cycle continues for much longer, it can easily weather it with its mountain of cash and still profitable operations. Once everything begins to turn around, I expect earnings to rebound massively and share price to begin its ascension, although it already seems to be starting, as its share price is already up 34% in one year, compared to the SPDR® S&P 500 ETF Trust’s (SPY) 15%.

Overall, I think there is very little that could have been done by the management team to improve operations if the demand was just not there. You really cannot force anyone to use your services and products if the customers don’t need them. It is part of the cycle process, and currently, ACLS is experiencing the worst, which may not end just yet.

Comments on the Outlook

Upcoming Earnings

Analysts are expecting the company to make $1.12 and $0.82 in adjusted and GAAP EPS, respectively, on $215m in sales. That translates to around 15% y/y decline, but a slight increase sequentially. Less than 1%, but it’s something. The management’s guidance for the upcoming quarter is more or less in line with the consensus. They are being quite conservative given the ongoing digestion and a pending Veeco merger, which is understandable. There’s no need to be too positive if there is nothing positive to report. Looking back over the last ten quarters, the company consistently beat estimates on top and bottom lines, which indicates to me that the management is being too conservative or analysts are just that much more pessimistic regarding the company’s prospects. Nevertheless, we could see another decent beat on the top and bottom lines coming up.

I’m not expecting any miracles in the upcoming earnings results, but I would like to get some comments regarding what the management sees for 2026. Any comments regarding capacity, demand softness, and the bright spots for the year would be great.

I will be looking out for comments regarding the main revenue contributors, like industrial and auto, but I am expecting quite subdued performance for 2026 still. I used to think that EVs would see a much-needed recovery in 2023 and growth in 2024, only to be proved wrong. Now, we will most likely soon get the EV sales growth for ’25, which should be around 22%, and growth for 2026 is projected to be around 13%, so I want to hear what the management has to say about this development.

What I am really curious to hear about is how the company is going to take advantage of the current HBM, NAND, and DRAM situation, which created a lot of bottlenecks and made many companies a lot more profitable, the main ones being Western Digital (WDC), Sandisk (SNDK), and Micron (MU). I have a position in MU, and I covered all of them in the recent past, giving them strong buys as the demand in the data center space for their products has been absolutely ridiculous. All of them are already out of capacity for the next year, and new capacity is to be built as early as late ’27, so this shortage is going to persist.

ACLS benefits from this surge, too, because ion implanters, which are a critical doping step for wafers, are going to be in a lot more demand. Unfortunately, this business segment is not the biggest in terms of total revenue, so it makes sense why the company hasn’t seen such a massive explosion in revenues. It just doesn’t work like that. However, I want to hear what the management has to say regarding it anyway, because they did say DRAM/HBM is a bright spot for the company. Something that will help its bookings to recover slightly and offset the overall power and general mature digestion.

I want to also hear how the merger is progressing with Veeco (VECO) and what kind of synergies investors expect in the long run. The merger is expected to close sometime in the second half of the year. This move should unlock around $45m run-rate cost savings, and should be non-GAAP EPS accretive in year 1, with cross-selling and tech integration. The merger complements ACLS’s implant doping business with VECO’s anneal, ion beam, and packaging, which, according to the press release, unlocks over $5B TAM in AI/power/packaging segments, creating a factory workflow combo.

Good time to invest in Axcellis?

At the moment, I don’t think so. The company’s operations continue to deteriorate in terms of margins, and I see no end in sight for now. I am also afraid that the demand for HBM, NAND, and DRAM is just not sufficient enough to make me jump in, as I don’t think there is a turnaround just yet. The company is more attractive now than it was before, but I still feel it will be quite the underperformer in 2026, given how other business segments continue their subpar performance. The downturn continues to affect it, but once that turns around, the company will be an attractive buy once again. I am looking forward to hearing what the management has to say about 2026.

data.title: Vistra's Growth Trajectory: Why The Stock Is Poised To Rise, But Better Plays Exist
data.description:
Investment thesis

Vistra Corp’s (VST) recent acquisitions ensure sustained growth over the next few years. With an expected EBITDA of above $7.4 billion by 2027 and debt reduction, my conservative fair value for the stock is estimated at $200 per share.

Background

Vistra Corp is an US integrated retail electricity and power generation company operating in 18 states and serving nearly 5 million residential, commercial, and industrial customers across through several retail brands. Moreover, the company operates one of the largest generation fleets in the country, with about 44 GW of capacity across natural gas, nuclear, coal, solar, and battery storage assets

While most of Vistra’s capacity and production come from gas, in 2024 it acquired Energy Harbor and its four nuclear plants, positioning itself as the second largest nuclear fleet in the US and representing around 20% of Vistra energy production.

Over the last few years most of Vistra’s growth has come from acquisitions, and it appears that this tendency will continue in the future. This month the company announced the acquisition of Cogentrix Energy and its 10 natural gas plants with around 5,500 MW capacity. Although the company is also investing in organic growth, such as with the construction of two natural gas plants with 860 MW capacity.

To be able to fund this growth and maintain visibility, the company hedges and sells under PPAs its energy, as the recent PPA signed with Meta (META) for 2,600 MW. This allows to have clear cash flow visibility until at least 2027 and plan the capital allocation.

Until the year end 2027 the company expects to be able to allocate around $10 billion, distributing about one third of it to shareholders, with the remainder going to growth and debt reduction.

The company plans to base most of shareholder’s returns on buybacks, paying a meager $0.22 quarterly dividend, representing around a 0.5% yield. While I consider Vistra will continue to increase its quarterly dividend, the company is targeting around $300 million per year in dividends, a number that it is already exceeding, suggesting that future increases will be modest.

It seems that the company is using an automatic repurchase program and buying shares regardless of the stock price. I would prefer a more active approach. For example, increasing buybacks now instead of in Q3 when the stock was above $200. Buying back the same amount with the current price would be $30 million cheaper. Alternatively, allocating the buyback budget to dividends instead could increase the yield to around 2%, which is more attractive than the current level.

Financial Position and Valuation

Vistra has a comfortable financial position with $505 million in cash and nearly $19 billion in debt. While debt may seem elevated, it is standard for the sector, and its net debt/EBITDA ratio stands at 2.6x, below the company’s 3x target. Moreover, most of this debt is due after 2030, giving plenty of time to refinance it when needed. In fact, the company recently issued new debt at very attractive rates.

Regarding valuation, the company’s EBITDA guidance for 2026 is between $6.8 and $7.6 billion, representing an increase of nearly 25% over 2025. These numbers are mostly secure as almost all of the 2026 generation production is hedged. For 2027, the company expects a lower growth but still an EBITDA around $7.6 billion.

Assuming that the company achieves the lower end of the 2027 range, which I believe will be increased next year, that uses $2 billion to reduce debt and that maintains a 12x EV/EBITDA, the stock’s price should be $213 per share.

This is why my fair value is around $200 per share, representing more than a 20% upside but difficult to justify repurchases above that level without more positive assumptions.

While I’m long the stock, I prefer AES Corporation (AES), where I expect around 100% upside and strong growth.

Risks

Vistra has several risks. In addition to operational risks, particularly in its nuclear facilities, I consider its main risk to be commodity and electricity price volatility. While most of Vistra’s generation is hedged or sold under PPAs, volatility in natural gas or power prices could still affect margins, especially beyond 2027 when current hedges expire.

Another risk is the credit risk. While current leverage is manageable and the company is reducing its interest expenses. The company needs to refinance its debt from time to time. If credit market is closed due to market turmoil, or persistent higher interest rate, refinancing costs could increase considerably.

Conclusion

Vistra Corp presents consistent growth, and its acquisitions have continued over the last two years, suggesting this trend will persist in the future. Debt remains below the company’s target, which means that the company could continue to acquire companies at attractive valuations to grow.

With the expected EBITDA growth and assuming modest debt reduction, the stock could be valued above $200, representing around 20% upside.

data.title: General Dynamics Can Still Shoot Higher From Here
data.description:
The last couple of days have been a bit rough for shareholders of General Dynamics Corporation (GD). Shares of the company are down around 4%. This happens to coincide with management announcing financial results covering the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Interestingly, revenue and earnings per share both came in above what analysts were hoping to see. And based on the data currently available, it is looking as though the future will be bright. After all, backlog is growing nicely, and relative to other similar firms, shares are trading on the cheap.

Even though the last couple of days have been a bit rough, the stock is still up 9% since I called the company a "Buy" in September of last year. This is approximately double the 4.7% rise that the S&P 500 saw over the same period. Normally, I wouldn't be bullish on a company that's this pricey. But its relative valuation is attractive, and I consider it a stable cash cow of an enterprise that should continue to thrive in the current environment. Ultimately, I would argue that it justifies maintaining a very soft "Buy" rating right now.

A Great Company at an Okay Price

On Jan. 28, the management team at General Dynamics announced financial results for the last quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Revenue for the company came in strong at $14.38 billion. In addition to representing an increase of 7.8% over the $13.34 billion the company reported a year earlier, it also happened to be $618 million above what analysts were hoping to see. This wasn't the only outperformance that the company achieved. Earnings per share grew from $4.15 to $4.17. Although this was not a large increase, it did exceed what analysts were expecting to the tune of $0.06. But unfortunately, because of a decline in the number of shares outstanding, it actually translated to a small drop in net profits from $1.15 billion to $1.14 billion. As the chart above illustrates, other profitability metrics for the company were mixed. So I wouldn't necessarily say that it was a great quarter.

2025 as a whole was better. In the chart above, you can see revenue, profits, and cash flows for this window of time. Sales of $52.55 billion exceeded the $47.72 billion the company reported the year prior. Net profits and almost every cash flow metric for the company also improved. The one exception to this was operating cash flow, which dipped ever so slightly from $4.96 billion to $4.93 billion. But if that's the only negative that we saw for the year in its entirety, I will take it. The fact that net income and other cash flow metrics improved sequentially is great. And it is a testament to the quality of the business.

When we drill down deeper, we get some rather impressive results. For instance, we need only look at the Marine Systems segment. This part of the company produces nuclear-powered submarines, as well as certain ships. It also offers up goods and services associated with all of this. Examples of the vessels that it rolls out include Columbia-class ballistic missile submarines and Virginia-class attack submarines. For those not familiar, Columbia-class ballistic missile submarines fall under a 12-boat program being run by the U.S. Navy. These are high-quality vessels, with the first boat not expected to be delivered until 2028. The objective here is to replace the current Ohio-class ballistic missile submarine fleet as it nears the end of its service life. And the total backlog associated with this initiative is worth around $125 billion. Virginia-class submarines are a bit different. But in essence, the company is working on 14 of these that will be delivered through the year 2032.

Revenue under this segment jumped to $4.82 billion for the quarter. That was 21.7% above the $3.96 billion reported a year earlier. And with that rise in revenue came a surge in operating earnings from $200 million to $345 million. Management chalked this expansion up primarily to the submarine programs and its electric boat business. And with the rise in revenue came a greater ability for the company to capture margin. This can be chalked up to greater productivity caused by scale and an emphasis by management to be principled in their operations. A lot of this also seems to stem from big investments that the company has made over the years, including those associated with its electric boat business aimed at enabling significant increases in output.

There were other areas of growth also, but they were small by comparison. The Combat Systems segment, for instance, saw revenue expand from $2.40 billion to $2.54 billion. And with that, operating earnings popped 7% from $356 million to $381 million. The company seems to be benefiting from significant awards when it comes to munitions. But it also saw strength when it came to its wheeled and tracked vehicle programs at European Land Systems.

Meanwhile, the Aerospace segment reported a 1.2% increase in revenue from $3.74 billion to $3.79 billion. This is a space that is very interesting to me. Most of my efforts when it comes to the aviation space involve the commercial sector. And what we have seen since the end of the COVID-19 pandemic has been an explosion in air travel, taking passenger flights up to all-time highs. Management did say that they saw some attractive demand when it came to Gulfstream aircraft, with services associated with those proving to be a boon. Jet aviation revenue also improved. Beyond that, we really don't know much about what went on there. However, a lot of what's going on successfully seems to be associated with its G700 and G800 platforms. But unfortunately, because of certain one-time events back in 2024, operating earnings actually contracted from $585 million to $481 million.

The only weak spot that the company saw from a revenue standpoint ended up being its Technologies segment. Revenue essentially flatlined at $3.24 billion. But technically, it was about a $2 million drop in sales. More problematic ended up being its profitability, with operating earnings contracting from $318 million to $290 million. Management specified that the market this segment operates in has been "extremely difficult." Some of this is because of government or quasi-government initiatives aimed at cutting waste. Beyond that, however, we don't have any substantive details regarding the bottom line performance of this segment.

For 2026 in its entirety, management is optimistic. Revenue is expected to come in at between $54.3 billion and $54.8 billion. At the midpoint, this would be a year-over-year increase of 3.8%. And with that, we should expect earnings per share of between $16.10 and $16.20. If management buys back stock or issues stock, actual net profits could vary. But as things stand today, we are looking at a net income of around $4.42 billion at the midpoint. In all likelihood, this would mean adjusted operating cash flow in the range of $5.18 billion and EBITDA of $6.60 billion.

These estimates, as well as the historical results that the company achieved for the last two completed fiscal years, give us the ability to see how shares are currently priced. In the chart above, you can see precisely what I mean. On an absolute basis, the stock is actually quite pricey. Normally, I would be more neutral on a business like this. But in the chart below, you can see how the enterprise stacks up against five other similar firms. Without exception, General Dynamics is the cheapest of the group.

To illustrate for you just how affordable the stock is on a relative basis, I created the next chart below. In it, you can see how much upside the stock would experience if it were to trade at the multiple of the cheapest of these five competitors. The chart also shows how much upside we would be looking at if the firm were to trade at the average. In the worst-case scenario, we are looking at an upside of only about 3.1%. But when we shift to the average approach, we are looking at a pretty consistent range of between 67.2% and 69.6%. This is compelling, to say the least.

We should not ignore one other fact, which is that future revenue growth is highly probable irrespective of what management said for 2026. This is because the backlog for the company continues to expand. The chart below shows exactly what I am talking about. The firm ended the final quarter of 2020 live with $118.05 billion of backlog. That's an increase of 30.3%, or $27.45 billion, compared to the $90.60 billion the company reported a year earlier. With the U.S. government looking to spend even more on defense, with the current administration pushing for a military budget of $1.5 trillion for the next year, which would represent a more than 50% increase over what we are currently spending, the overall picture for the company should continue to improve from this point on.

Takeaway

The data provided indicates to me that, on the whole, General Dynamics is a pretty attractive prospect. No, the firm is not as cheap as I would like it to be. But its relative valuation is compelling, and financial performance is solid. Cash flows are coming in strong, and I see no reason why that trend should not continue moving forward. This combination of actors justifies, in my view, a soft "Buy" rating for the stock.

data.title: Dumping U.S. Dollar: Why My Dry Powder Is Now In Swiss Francs
data.description:
Over the last few months I have mentioned multiple times my intention to pile up some cash, and this is what I did. I don’t think there are currently many opportunities to seize; therefore, I am waiting on the sidelines. However, as the cash pile gets bigger, my mind has started to wonder whether or not it is a good choice to hold most of my liquidity in US Dollar. And this is what this article is about: why did I decide to rely less on the US Dollar (USD)? And above all, what led me to rely on the Swiss Franc (CHF)?

Why I want to reduce my exposure to the USD

The first reason why I wanted to reduce my exposure to the USD is purely technical/personal. I live in Italy and euro is my reference currency; however, most of my investments are in US companies. Furthermore, I am paid in USD, but my daily expenses are in euros: the exchange rate can negatively influence my spending capacity. And this is exactly what happened since the new administration took office.

The USD depreciated against the euro by 13%, a double hit to both my earnings and my investments. Imagine what would happen if the EUR/USD reached 1.30 or 1.50. Can you rule it out completely? After the explosive rise in gold and silver prices, I believe that nothing is impossible, especially if it has already occurred (2004-2014).

For a foreign investor, a depreciating USD has more direct consequences compared to a US investor. You immediately notice it.

For US investors, 2025 was a great year for the domestic stock market; for European investors, the situation was quite different: the USD depreciation has almost totally offset the gains. Therefore, it is not a coincidence that, after a long time, Emerging Markets are widely outperforming the S&P 500 index: investors feel the need to diversify from the USD.

Overall, in my specific case, I believe it is definitely not worth it to keep my cash in USD, as I am already too exposed due to my investments and job; I wouldn’t be surprised if other foreign investors might decide to do the same. And to some extent, I believe this reasoning can also apply to US individuals.

Previously, I stated that a depreciating USD has visible and direct implications for foreign individuals; in fact, the S&P 500 total return in euros was 14% lower in 2025. However, even though more hidden, US individuals can also be damaged by a weak domestic currency due to the rising cost of imports.

Consider that the US trade deficit will probably hit $1 trillion in 2025 despite tariffs, and a weaker USD means importing inflation. If a US company wants to import something from a European one, in terms of USD, it will cost about 15% more than last year. On top of this, you also have to include the import tariffs. Inflation is a sneaky tax for US households, especially for those with low incomes. The more the USD depreciates, the more import inflation becomes significant, and your purchasing power declines. Japan knows something about it.

After three decades of no inflation/deflation, Japan is now dealing with an inflation rate systematically higher than before. It is not a worrisome level, but consider that a whole Japanese generation doesn’t even know what it feels like to lose purchasing power. The aspect you need to focus on is not the 2% inflation rate but what has led to this unexpected outcome. When you ask yourself this question, the answer is pretty straightforward in my opinion: import inflation, since the yen lost 50% of its value against other major currencies in a couple of years.

The USD is just at the beginning of its depreciating path, which is why the import inflation hasn’t yet materialized. Am I 100% sure about this? I can’t be; nobody can predict the future with certainty, but as of now I have multiple arguments to support this thesis. All of them converge on the same conclusion: the reputation of the USD is deteriorating; therefore, it will lose value. Let’s start with the first one.

USD weaponization

Following Russia’s attack on Ukraine, the US and its allies decided to freeze Russian foreign exchange reserves in their jurisdictions. All of a sudden, Russia lost about $300 billion worth of debt securities denominated in USD and euros. It is not up to me to say whether it was the right thing to do or not, but that move has set an important precedent.

If you use your currency as a weapon, you are basically disincentivizing foreign countries to hold it. To put it simply, this is what is going on in their mind: “If I don’t get along with the US, then they can freeze overnight all my USD reserves in their jurisdictions.” And nowadays, it is not so hard not to get along with the US. Tariffs, Greenland, threatening Iran, and downplaying Canada and the EU are just some of the topics that are fueling the geopolitical pressure.

For these reasons, since 2022, worldwide central banks have decided to start buying gold and dumping USD. They want to hold an asset that can’t be weaponized, and that’s a major concern for the US economy. Lower USD in foreign central banks means less demand for US Treasuries, therefore higher interest rates to pay on the ongoing fiscal deficits. The shift is already happening, and it's accelerating.

For the first time since 1996, gold holdings exceeded U.S. Treasury holdings as a percentage of international reserves, and I don’t believe that something could change anytime soon. Central banks are buying gold like never before: the average monthly purchases amount to 60 tons, 3 times more than in early 2022. On the other hand, who is buying US Treasuries?

Major foreign official holders are not increasing their exposure to T-Bonds and Notes. Gold is preferred despite its skyrocketing price.

Fed's loss of independence

Regarding this topic, I discussed it thoroughly in a recent article. In a nutshell, I believe that interfering with the proper function of the monetary policy is one of the biggest mistakes a country can make. There is a reason why central banks should be politically independent, and throughout history we have had lots of negative examples to not follow. One of the most recent ones is Turkey.

Turkey has never been considered a rock-solid economy with a strong currency; in fact, the 2005-2020 average inflation rate has been 9.44%. In 2021, when almost the entire world was struggling with high inflation, Turkey’s inflation rate reached 30%, and an urgent maneuver was needed. When inflation gets so out of control, the first thing that everyone would do is to hike interest rates significantly, but this is not what happened. Going against any empirical evidence, the Turkish leader Erdoğan decided that the best thing to do at that moment was to lower interest rates and pressured the central bank to follow its vision. The outcome of this choice is obvious.

The inflation rate reached almost triple, and the Turkish people’s savings have been wiped out. Now the situation is normalizing as the central bank decided to hike interest rates. Could all this have been avoided? Hard to say. We all know that lowering rates was the worst thing to do back then, but I don't think it's easy to stand up to political pressure.

Could the USD follow the same path as the Turkish lira? Of course not; it would be absurd to even think about it. However, I’ll tell you what is not helping USD credibility and could lead to a constant depreciation against the major global currencies:

  • Firing Erika McEntarfer, commissioner of the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS), for the following motivation, according to US President Trump: “rigged jobs figures to make the Republicans, and ME, look bad".
  • The DOJ starting a criminal investigation into Fed Chair Jerome Powell. According to Powell, the primary reason for this investigation is not following the President’s will: “The threat of criminal charges is a consequence of the Federal Reserve setting interest rates based on our best assessment of what will serve the public, rather than following the preferences of the President.”
  • Appointing a new Fed Chair under the influence of external forces. This is what President Trump recently announced: "By the way, when we have a great Fed chairman -- I think we’re going to have one -- I’ll announce it pretty soon. You’ll see rates come down a lot."

Overall, we will probably see a much lower Fed Funds Rate when the GDP growth for Q4 2025 is expected to be 5.40% and the inflation rate is still ~3%. Meanwhile, import inflation is increasing, the fiscal deficit never goes down, and just recently, oil price is going up. What can go wrong?

Fiscal policy is out of control

This is the last, and maybe the most important reason why the USD is losing its credibility: the US economy has become addicted to growing fiscal deficits.

The 50-year average deficit as a % of GDP is 3.80%, but the US government is systematically above this threshold. The federal deficit in FY2025 stands at 5.90%, a figure that has been exceeded only 8 times in history: to overcome the GFC (2009-2012), the pandemic (2020, 2021) and in 2023,2024.

I get the point of spending more during the GFC and the pandemic, but the real question here is about the last three years and the next ones. From 2023 to 2025, the fiscal deficit has been huge for no reason in particular and the forecasts don’t show a turnaround: doing too much today limits the room for maneuver when a real crisis hits.

If you need to constantly print money to sustain the economy, it is probably not as strong as it looks. It doesn’t make sense to avoid a recession by increasing improperly the fiscal deficit: sooner or later the economy will pay a high price.

Actually, it is already starting to pay this high price if you know where to look for it.

It doesn’t matter if the Fed is lowering the short-term rate, the long-term maturity yields are getting higher. The bond market is increasingly distrustful about US Treasuries, and that’s a huge problem if you have to refinance $9 trillion this year. Interest payments on national debt are 11.90% higher than last year and they are currently the second-largest spending category for the federal government; nobody is doing anything to bring this spending category down, quite the opposite. According to the Congressional Budget Office (CBO), if the market conditions remain the same, from 2025 to 2035 the US will pay cumulative net interests of $13.80 trillion; $1.80 trillion just in 2035. And in my opinion, this is not even the worst-case scenario.

Both the bond market and FX have already started to react to these excessive fiscal deficits; the stock market is showing some cracks abroad. On the surface, the latter seems in great shape and close to an all-time high, but that is just because we are considering it in USD. If we don’t take the currency into account in our investments, let’s just invest in the Turkish stock market (XU100). The chart seems even more bullish: from 2018 to date, it delivered a price return of 1,000%.

It was a great hedge against inflation for Turkish people, but definitely not worth it for US investors: the price return in USD has been 0%.

Clearly, this example is a hyperbole that has only an explicative purpose. However, the US stock market is also showing a significant return difference based on the underlying currency.

Over the last 12 months, the S&P500 return in USD has been +15%. Except for JPY, all the other currencies are experiencing a much lower price return; for the Swiss Franc is even negative.

Therefore, just because a stock market is at an all-time high in its domestic currency, it doesn’t mean that it is doing well. It is not just a matter of price, but also a matter of currency. As you can see, FX, the bond market and the stock market are all interconnected, that’s why an investor should closely watch each one of them.

Why replace USD with CHF?

In the previous section I have explained my reasons behind the intention of not holding anymore a significant amount of liquidity in USD. At the same time, I don’t trust the euro either. It is getting involved in complex situations that can worsen the cohesion between EU members (Greenland, how to respond to US tariffs, issues with NATO). Of course, I don’t trust the yen either as it is widely depreciating even against USD; the Japanese debt is a big concern. So, what is the solution? Maybe a small country in the Alps with a population comparable to New York City. What is so special about Switzerland? Well, in my opinion is by far the most solid economy in the world, and its currency (Swiss Franc, CHF) is designed to appreciate over time against all the other currencies thanks to a perfect management of the inflation rate.

Over the last 25 years, the highest inflation level was 2.84% in 2022, but as you probably know, all the other countries suffered a way higher inflation rate. The purchasing power of the Swiss has remained intact over the years, something that we don’t find in other major economies. This divergence is the main reason behind the systematic CHF appreciation.

From 2000 on, the CHF appreciated against the most important currencies by triple digit (euro “just” 74%). And if you look at the recent period, the CHF is acting as a shield against the debasement trade.

It is not a coincidence that CHF greatly appreciated against USD and JPY; double-digit returns just by holding liquidity in CHF. Both US and Japan are facing severe debt sustainability problems, and the market is doubtful on how they will solve them. If they keep running huge fiscal deficits and printing money, the value of their currencies can only go down. On the other hand, Switzerland is in a totally opposite situation.

The Swiss Debt/GDP ratio is just above 30% and it is expected to remain stable over the next 5 years. If you are wondering how it is possible to have such a low Debt/GDP ratio, the answer is pretty simple: in early 2000s it was introduced a debt break rule. Basically, Switzerland is allowed to run a deficit only during recessions; that implies that most of the times runs a fiscal surplus.

The secret lies entirely here, they simply understand that saving money during economic booms makes the difference when bad times come. They know when to save, when to spend and how to spend.

At this point someone might downplay Switzerland’s achievements because of the very small population (9 million), after all it is way harder to run a country with hundreds of millions of people. That is true, but I believe that Switzerland’s foresight is the most important factor here. The debt break rule has been approved by 85% of voters, which means that most of the population shares the anti-cyclical economic approach. From 2023 to 2025, the US has done exactly the opposite and that is not a surprise to me. In the current state, the US economy needs to significantly cut the fiscal deficit, but who would vote someone who wants to raise taxes and reduce government spending?

Overall, the CHF has proved to be the most solid currency in the world, but this doesn’t mean that you should all convert your savings in CHF. I think it is important to understand that this strategic allocation is not always worth it; as every investment, it depends on your expectations.

The biggest downside of CHF is that it doesn’t provide any risk-free interest. Switzerland is so solid that the 10Y bond yield stands at 0.22% and the 1Y bond yield is slightly negative. Unlike USD and euro, there isn’t a money market that provides you an annual risk-free interest of 2%-3.50%. This implies that, to justify your investment in CHF, the latter should appreciate against USD/euro at least by 5% each year. I might be wrong, but in my opinion this target is easily achievable, as I no longer trust the US debt trajectory and I am concerned about the cohesion between EU members.

Finally, as a final point supporting my thesis, the US Dollar Index has recently broken below a historic support level: we are heading to uncharted territory.

Conclusion

In an economic context where geopolitics gets the front page of newspapers, the role of safe-haven assets becomes increasingly important. The CHF is currently the most solid currency in the world as it belongs to a country that knows how to manage the debt properly; it is not a given nowadays. Furthermore, Switzerland is not part of EU or NATO, and its historical neutrality substantially reduces the CHF geopolitical risk.

Of course, I can’t totally eliminate the geopolitical risk of the USD. Companies like Meta, Google, Visa and McDonald’s will continue to have a major role in my portfolio; on the other hand, I am not planning to add any Swiss company. In other words, the US will continue to have the best companies in the world, but a more fragile currency than in the past. The CHF fills this gap.

My main goal is to accumulate as much CHF as possible, and then buy US companies once their valuations (and the USD) get significantly cheaper. I believe that the USD depreciation process has just begun, I want to be ready to seize the opportunity when it arises. It is not certain that it will arise, but I believe numbers are on my side.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Eli Lilly: Obesity Pricing For Access Tradeoff A Net Positive
data.description:
Eli Lilly (LLY) entered 2026 with strong momentum across the board. Its share price has pulled back slightly recently but it is still near all-time highs, the pipeline has been delivering significant wins, and the commercial business has been outperforming expectations. It was, therefore, not surprising to see the stock appreciate 35% since my last update in September 2025.

When expectations are high, a company needs to execute, and that is what Eli Lilly has been doing over the last few quarters, and years too. I have spent a lot of time covering Eli Lilly’s pipeline developments in the last few articles, and I think the commercial outlook matters more in the near-term, and that is the primary focus of today’s article.

Price Cuts for Obesity Drugs for Expanded Access—a Big Net Positive

Eli Lilly has made important tradeoffs in recent months. Through an agreement with the U.S. government, it has committed to reduce the price of Zepbound and to launch the yet-to-be approved oral small molecule GLP-1 agonist orforglipron at an affordable price in exchange for expanded access and tariff exemptions.

The prices of Zepbound and orforglipron will also be lower for self-pay patients.

Price cuts are not ideal, but I believe this move will be very net positive for Eli Lilly, and for Novo Nordisk (NVO), which has done a similar deal for Wegovy.

The lower prices of obesity drugs have two significant benefits:

  1. They secure Medicare coverage of Zepbound and the same should happen to orforglipron when it gets approved. This is a sales channel that was previously not available and the $50 monthly copay limit should also help with affordability.
  2. The lowered list prices for self-pay patients should significantly expand this market. Zepbound now starts as low as $299 a month and up to $449 for the highest dose, and orforglipron will start at $149 and max out at $399 per monthly prescription.

On the negative side, these price cuts could flow through to the commercial channel and result in additional pricing pressure from PBMs.

In any case, obesity is becoming the largest pharmaceutical market ever, and penetration rates are still very low. I have not seen Eli Lilly’s estimates, but Novo Nordisk has a presentation slide that paints a great picture of the potential of the obesity market – Novo estimates that only 4 million patients are on anti-obesity medicines (‘AOMs’) today, which represents less than 0.5% market penetration versus roughly 7% penetration of GLP-1 medicines in the diabetes market. And both markets are growing.

Expanded access in the U.S. and international expansion should go a long way to increase penetration rates, especially now that Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk are not supply constrained anymore.

Commercial Outlook for 2026

So, how should the above-mentioned price cuts for expanded access translate to 2026 numbers?

Making predictions is a tough task and there are moving parts and unknowns that could swing the results in either direction. For example, the timing of orforglipron’s approval is a big swing factor – Eli Lilly has gained and used the Priority Review voucher which should translate to an early second quarter launch, but there are no guarantees with the FDA and the review could be extended for one reason or another.

The adoption curves across the government and self-pay channels are also not easy to model, and the recent launch of oral Wegovy is a potential headwind for Zepbound due to the convenience of an oral drug versus an injectable, and it also has a head start over orforglipron.

With that in mind, I expect Eli Lilly to deliver 2026 revenues well above the current $77.5 billion consensus. Not only based on the strong growth outlook for the obesity business, but because Q4 2025 revenues should not be too far from $77.5 billion on an annualized basis. I expect Q4 2025 revenues to reach or exceed $18.5 billion and this is already $74 billion annualized, and this should represent a strong foundation for 2026.

I expect 2026 revenues to be in the $83-85 billion range, which translates to approximately 30% growth at the mid-point of the range. And if some or all of the mentioned factors swing to the positive side, I can see a scenario where Y/Y growth rates remain in the mid- to high-40s throughout the year. 40% revenue growth would bring the full-year 2026 revenue to $90 billion.

However, I do not expect management to be this aggressive when they provide guidance next week, simply because they do not have to and I expect them to start lower with a range that is somewhere between $78 billion and $80 billion and increase the full-year guidance throughout the year.

I should add that I have relatively modest expectations for the rest of the product portfolio. My baseline assumption is 5-10% sales growth in 2026, as market share losses of larger drugs like Verzenio could offset the progress of newer product launches such as Ebglyss, Jaypirca, Kisunla, and Omvoh.

And lastly, while I care the most about topline growth, earnings growth should outpace revenue growth in 2026 due to the scale of the obesity business which does not need corresponding increases in sales and marketing expenses. The analyst consensus is already at 40.8% EPS growth for 2026 based on 21.4% revenue growth, and if my revenue estimates are met, EPS growth should be 45-50%, or better.

Recent Pipeline Updates

Eli Lilly’s pipeline has continued to produce positive late-stage results.

Orforglipron showed weight loss maintenance following induction with Zepbound is possible.

Retatrutide generated positive phase 3 results in patients with osteoarthritis pain, showing that significant weight loss can reduce pain from osteoarthritis.

Amylin analog eloralintide captured attention with up to 20.6% weight loss in obese patients after 48 weeks of treatment, though higher doses were met with worsening tolerability. Still, the 3/6/9mg dose titration regimen delivered 16.7% weight loss with acceptable tolerability and the results set the stage for phase 3 trials of eloralintide monotherapy and combination trials with tirzepatide and other pipeline candidates.

I was impressed by the positive topline results of the combination of Taltz (IL-17A antibody) and tirzepatide in patients with psoriatic arthritis. These results showed that losing weight with tirzepatide while being on Taltz produces a significant treatment benefit versus Taltz alone.

I believe this could lead to the addition of tirzepatide as a combination partner in an increasing number of indications where obesity is considered a comorbidity.

Eli Lilly also announced two small acquisitions of Adverum and Ventyx, and both expand the company’s pipeline outside of obesity. Ventyx’s small molecule NLRP3 inhibitor VTX3232 has broad potential in cardiometabolic, inflammatory, and neurodegenerative disorders. VTX3232 generated good biomarker data last year in combination with semaglutide, but it did not show an impact on weight loss.

Conclusion

2026 should be another year of strong growth for Eli Lilly, driven by the expanded access for tirzepatide and by the launch of orforglipron.

There are a lot of moving parts that make 2026 estimates a tougher-than-usual task, but my base case scenario is Y/Y revenue growth of approximately 30% versus the current 21% consensus, and the bullish case would be a topline growth rate greater than 35%. Similarly, I expect 45-50% EPS growth, or better, in 2026.

The near-term risks are a delayed approval and launch of orforglipron, worse-than-expected uptake in government and self-pay channels, worse-than-expected pricing erosion and oral Wegovy doing better at the expense of Zepbound and orforglipron. Longer-term, Eli Lilly should expect significantly increased competition in the obesity and type 2 diabetes markets as nearly every big pharma company and potentially a few smaller companies will have product launches by the end of the decade.

data.title: HOOY: Attractive Entry Before Robinhood's Q4 Earnings (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

When it comes to single-stock option ETFs, I believe that timing is very important. These are high-risk instruments that can be disastrous if held through unfavorable price momentum. So when I previously covered the YieldMax HOOD Option Income Strategy ETF (HOOY), I issued a hold rating since the fund traded near all-time highs at the time. Since my last coverage, HOOY's share price has declined by more than 34% and now sits near its all-time lows. Therefore, I wanted to revisit the ETF's current appeal now that the momentum has shifted.

Looking at the performance since inception, we can see that HOOY's share price has now eroded by about 17.7%. As Robinhood Markets (HOOD) continues to see its share price decline, HOOY's momentum has followed. However, the inclusion of all distributions takes the total return up to 59.4% over the same time frame. The main appeal of HOOY at this time is the high starting dividend yield of 54.5%. The fund also issues payouts on a weekly basis, which can be ideal for high risk income investors. However, there are some clear tradeoffs that investors should consider before initiating a position just because of the high yield.

Now that HOOY has fallen from its highs, we can see how HOOY's price erosion has really increased over recent months. This is a common threat to many of the different YieldMax funds, which makes it a difficult fund to buy and hold through all market conditions. However, I believe that HOOD has some potential catalysts to help it regain some of its positive momentum. As a result, HOOY should be able to partially participate in this price upside and the distributions will rise as the underlying option strategy produces larger premiums. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that HOOY implements to generate the amplified income needed to support distributions.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, HOOY now has net assets of $146M and an expense ratio of 0.99% of assets. As the share price has fallen, we can see how the underlying assets under management has also declined from its highs. Since distributions remain so high, they may be actively accelerating the pace at which the assets are trending downward, which can be a serious threat to the fund if the downward momentum continues for a prolonged period of time.

HOOY implements an option writing strategy against HOOD and collects option premiums, which are then used to fund the high distributions. This fund's option writing strategy also allows HOOY to replicate the daily price change of HOOD over time. However, a synthetic approach comes with its own set of vulnerabilities that must be considered. The most notable risk is that a synthetic option writing strategy means that HOOY doesn't actually own any common shares of HOOD.

Since options are being written against an asset that the fund doesn't actually own, this increases price instability and means HOOY isn't structurally capable of seeing a meaningful level of price growth over time. When assessing the holdings within HOOY, we can see that the fund is most heavily made up of cash and U.S. Treasuries to maintain its liquidity. There are no common shares of HOOD within the fund, but there are active options:

  • HOOD US - 02/20/26 C$135
  • HOOD US - 01/30/26 C$117

As we can see, the active options have different expiration dates. Based on the holdings, HOOY will typically write options with an expiration that is ~30 days out. HOOY will typically implement an OTM (out-of-the-money) strategy, which means that the selected strike price will be slightly above the current market price of HOOD. This allows HOOY to partially participate in some upside price growth through bullish movements, but the price is still ultimately capped at whatever the selected strike price is.

So if HOOD currently trades around ~$104 per share, HOOY's option will only capture the price growth up to $117 per share. In a scenario where HOOD's price jumped sharply to $125 per share, HOOY would essentially miss out on all of the growth above the strike price of $117 per share. HOOY is essentially trading most of its upside growth in exchange for income that can be distributed out to shareholders.

Performance Insights & Outlook

Since the upside is capped, investors should anticipate that nearly all of their returns in HOOY will come from the fund's ability to maintain a high rate of distributions. However, HOOY's high dividend yield doesn't automatically translate to high total returns over time. If you are bullish on the outlook of HOOD, you would probably be better off simply owning the common shares of HOOD. As we can see below, HOOD's total return of 94.6% significantly outperforms HOOY's total return of 59.4% over the same time frame.

If the fund's share price continues to decline, this will eat into an investor's total return over time. Unfortunately, HOOY's structure means that a declining share price is almost a guarantee over a longer holding period. While the fund has capped upside growth, there is no structure in place to protect against downward markets. So this means that HOOY will feel the full impact of declines, while having trouble to recover on the upside movements that follow. Over time, this means that the fund's share price will typically trend downward. When you add in the high rate of distributions that are paid, which directly impact the share price, it is almost a certainty that HOOY will continue to trend downward.

However, this structure is by design and this is why I want to put emphasis on the fact that timing of entry is extremely important. I believe that the absolute best time to accumulate shares in HOOY is when the stock is trading flat and Robinhood may have a catalyst that takes it higher in the near-term. I believe that the outlook for HOOY can essentially be summarized by these three possible outcomes:

  1. Robinhood Markets Rises = HOOY will also partially participate in the growth. Depending on how strong the positive price momentum is, there's the chance that HOOY's distributions will also grow larger. However, HOOY is still likely to underperform HOOD over time due to the capped nature of its options.
  2. Robinhood Trades Flat = HOOY's share price may stay within a consistent trading range and when including distributions, HOOY has the potential to outperform. However, this relies on HOOD resisting any rapid downturns. Depending on how large the distributions are, NAV erosion may still occur.
  3. Robinhood Declines = HOOY is likely to experience capital erosion over time. The distributions may help offset these losses, but the share price is highly unlikely to recover unless there's a strong rise in HOOD. This is the phase we are currently in and we've already seen how the share price can rapidly erode over time.

HOOD is set to report its Q4 2025 earnings in mid-February and I believe there is a good chance that the report will be solid. HOOD's normalized EPS estimate comes in at $0.69 per share, with 12 positive revisions in the last 90 days. For reference, Wall Street analysts currently have an average price target of $148.53 per share, which indicates a potential upside of more than 42% from its current level. When HOOD reports its earnings, I believe there are some potential catalysts that the business has in its favor.

One of the most notable things to me would be the expansion of prediction markets. Robinhood has an easy way for users to interact with prediction markets and this will continue to expand as HOOD allows users to participate in almost anything, ranging from the outlook of the economy, politics, sports, and more. We also have the fact that HOOD finalized its acquisition of Bitstamp in mid-2025, which can help the business capitalize on growing interest within the crypto markets. While this may take a few quarters to materialize into higher earnings, it still serves as a forward looking catalyst that can be the next leg of growth. I think that any optimism in these operating channels can reverse HOOD's current price direction.

If I am correct, HOOY will be able to partially participate in HOOD's share price growth. As the price trends upward, HOOY will also be able to capture larger option premiums, assuming that volatility rises over the next quarter. While HOOY is still likely to underperform HOOD, this provides income investors with the opportunity to strategically capture a high rate of income and positive total returns.

Dividend Risks

The latest declared weekly distribution is $0.4542 per share, bringing the estimated annualized yield to ~54.5%. Since new distributions are being declared every week, the annualized yield may be different by the time you read this article. Looking at the dividend history below, we can see how the payouts have consistently shifted over time based on the underlying momentum of HOOD. So when HOOD was trading near all-time highs, HOOY was able to pay out much larger distributions since the option strategy was able to capture much larger premiums from the upswings. Conversely, the payouts have become smaller as the share price has declined. Please note that HOOY switched from monthly distributions to weekly distributions in late October.

Lower payouts are the primary risk for income investors over a longer holding period. If the underlying NAV declines, HOOY will have fewer assets that it can use to generate income with its options. Therefore, the distributions will be reduced over time to reflect the lower income generation. Before HOOY shifted to a weekly payout schedule, the fund previously issued a massive distribution of $6.90 per share back in July of 2025. Since the fund has fallen from its highs, the distributions have significantly declined.

Current $0.4542 per share payout X 4 weeks = $1.8168 payout

YieldMax sends out a distribution breakdown email every week and this is the clearest way to keep up with how much of the distribution is made up of return of capital. As we can see below, this payout was made up of 95.11% return of capital. While return of capital distributions are ideal because they are not directly taxed as income, they also directly reduce the fund's share price by an equivalent amount. Therefore, these distributions can actually cause the fund's share price to fall more rapidly than HOOD.

For example, HOOD traded at its all-time high on October 9, 2025. Since then, the share price has maintained a consistent downward trend. As we can see below, HOOY's share price has actually declined a lot quicker because of the return of capital distributions being paid. While HOOY's total return slightly outpaces HOOD, an investor's capital would still erode much quicker with HOOY.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am upgrading my rating on HOOY to a buy now that the share price has fallen from its highs. With HOOD's upcoming earnings, I believe that a positive report can spark optimism around the outlook of the business and help reverse momentum. Since HOOY's price growth is capped over a longer holding period, I believe that investors must strategically choose to time their entry and look for opportunities where the underlying stock may increase in value. While I have no expectation that HOOY will outperform HOOD over a longer holding period, I believe that a strategic position in HOOY can help us capture a high level of income and take advantage of volatility in the short-term.

data.title: ASML: Reality Check After Doubling To All-Time Highs (Downgrade)
data.description:
From A Contrarian “Buy” To A Stretched Valuation

Almost seven months ago, stocks of European chip-machine manufacturer ASML Holding N.V. (ASML) traded at half of today’s price. The stock had sold off on a lack of visibility into 2026 amid comments on unclear timing of customer capex. Nevertheless, management’s long-term vision until 2030 remained intact, prompting me to view this as nothing more than short-term uncertainty or conservative management of expectations, rather than a structural headwind. With the stock having traded around fair value at 626 euros, I took a contrarian stance, considering a “Buy” worthwhile.

Since then, ASML stock has almost doubled to 1,233 euros, now already pricing in successful years ahead and thus warranting a downgrade to “Hold” for the European chip-machine leader dominating the market for patterning equipment in wafer production.

Record Bookings Meet Improved Visibility

ASML released Q4-25 and FY-25 numbers this week with two especially positive readings.

First, 2026 is now officially expected to be a year of growth, with a mid-point revenue guidance of €37 billion ($43 billion at current rates), implying double-digit YoY growth. Even the more cautious guidance implies mid-single-digit growth, while the upper-end target range would even come with a growth expectation of 20%. Gross margin is expected to come in at 52%, roughly in line with the latest reports, and the 2030 outlook with a mid-point 10% top-line CAGR and gross margins expanding to a mid-point of 58% remains untouched.

Second, ASML’s book-to-bill ratio reached its highest value in at least seven quarters during Q4-25. Net bookings surged to an all-time high of €13.2 billion during the quarter against similarly record-breaking net system sales of €7.6 billion. This marks a notable reversal from book-to-bill ratios below 100% in prior quarters.

Although the number of lithography systems sold decreased YoY, the implied average selling price increased sharply, underscoring the company’s pricing power and leading standing in the industry. Service sales remained flattish during Q4, but with regards to an ever-increasing installed base, I expect this to be a transitory plateau.

Coming back to bookings, the massive order intake leads to a backlog of now €38.8 billion, significantly above 2025 revenues of €32.7 billion, supporting the newly ignited growth story. According to management, “many of our customers have shared a notably more positive assessment of the medium-term market situation” amid “more robust expectations of the sustainability of AI-related demand.”

But Valuation Is Now Ahead Of Its Time

And with the growth story intact, immediate shareholder returns appear almost negligible at this point. Dividend yield is 0.5%, and while an approved buyback program of up to €12 billion over the next three years at first sounds a lot in absolute terms, it would only reflect 1% of today’s market cap per year. Both lead us to valuation.

Based on a standardized approach (operating cash flow minus capex according to Seeking Alpha), TTM free cash flow of $13 billion increased 20% versus half a year ago. Although this is notably above my intentionally conservative estimates, at the same time, the share price and enterprise value almost doubled, naturally leading to significantly increased valuation.

An enterprise value of $551 billion now corresponds to 42x TTM free cash flow, translating to a 2.4% free cash flow yield (down from 3% last time). At the same time, my rounded WACC estimate for ASML remains elevated at 12% (even up from 11% last time) against the backdrop of increased beta and geopolitical instability, which ASML might be exposed to. As a result, the required growth residual is now approaching 10%, which appears highly ambitious.

Viewed differently, I argue that ASML’s share price is now roughly three years ahead of itself, whereas in July 2025, it was only reasonably ahead of itself by about one year.

As of July 2025, ASML’s 20-year average P/E and P/C were 26. These historical multiples would have been reached in roughly a year’s time on a forward basis.

As of today, ASML’s 20-year average P/E and P/C are similar at 27 and 26. Yet at today’s valuations (50x and 38x) and based on consensus estimates, it would require forward multiples three years out to see historical values again.

Takeaway

ASML is fully on track fundamentally. It became evident that blurry guidance from mid-2025 for 2026 was cautious but not detrimental to the company’s 2030 goals. As this short-term uncertainty was priced out again, ASML surged to all-time highs. At this point, however, the share price has reached valuation levels that historically marked somewhat of a mid-term peak.

As a result, I am downgrading this leading chip-machine manufacturer business to a “Hold” on the back of stretched valuation. Furthermore, the company is exposed to international political tensions, while at the same time, it might be an interesting non-U.S. play for investors looking for a company with low USD exposure. U.S. investors would also profit from FX effects on the stock if one wants to assume a continued weak USD against the Euro. I remain bullish about the company yet turn cautious on the stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Target: Best Thing To Do Now Is Do Nothing At All
data.description:
It was a gamble when I upgraded Target Corporation (TGT) to a buy rating and took a position in it in my previous coverage. Three months later, my view was not proven to be baseless considering its 12% returns in a relatively short period. But I advise you not to let your guard down, because TGT still lacks growth drivers to sustain further increases.

Now that it’s already above $100 again, it is essential that I revisit this stock and reassess my position here, whether or not to buy more or lighten them. Valuation is still reasonable, but it’s already getting closer to my previous TP or target price. Meanwhile, technicals are still bullish despite the recent downtrend.

Technicals: Overbuying and Profit-Taking, Not a Bearish Reversal

The short answer is no, if you ask me. Why? TGT is still generally increasing with higher highs and lows. The current price is still above the first key support of $93.99. So, the upward momentum still holds as of this writing. In addition, the stock price is still above the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which suggests that the current momentum is still stronger than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. It is also worth noting that the 50-day SMA is now increasing and has already crossed above the 200-day SMA.

This can tell us two things. First, recent market sentiments have remained generally optimistic. Second, bullish views in the past three months are stronger than in the past year. This can tell us that buyers saw an opportunity to enter during Q4 2025.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI can tell us more about the downtrend. The MACD line is trending downwards below the signal line. This means that selling volume is increasing, while buying volume is decreasing. The histogram is within negative territory, which implies that selling volume has outpaced buying volume recently.

Lastly, RSI is very interesting. This suggests that the current downtrend is not necessarily a reversal but the effect of the cautious stance of the market.

When the price reached its high of $111, the stock also became overbought. This opened a chance for profit-takers to take advantage of the situation. Until now, profit-taking is still evident and may continue until it becomes close to overselling. After all, the price is still above $100, and some investors may feel that it’s still high for their risk appetite.

I’d also like to highlight that its trend is still consistent with the price, so we cannot detect an early bearish divergence.

Valuation

As I looked into its valuation, things became more interesting. After focusing on the Dividend Discount Model, I proved that technicals are still in sync with valuation. If you remember, my target price (TP) in my previous coverage was $110.95 ($99.41 with a 10% risk consideration). When the stock price hit $111, the downtrend immediately began, which could tell us their strong correlation.

With that, I updated my Dividend Discount Model. The annualized dividend is still $4.56 based on the most recent quarterly payout of $1.14. Meanwhile, my estimated dividend growth rate is 7.88% based on the 2021-2025 CAGR. I also updated its cost of equity.

Risk premium is still 7.0% to be conservative and consistent with my previous coverage. But note that the risk-free rate is now 4.3% compared to 4.1% before. This is in line with the current 10-year treasury yield.

Lastly, my estimated Beta is now 1.08, which implies that TGT has become less volatile recently. Using all these numbers, my new TP is $114.57. This is higher than my previous TP. However, their gap is still narrow. Somehow, this justifies the market reaction and profit-taking when the price reached $110.

I also updated its price ratios and focused on P/E and P/S. Using P/E, the stock trades at 12.33x its EPS versus the five-year average of 18.87x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $155.66. We cannot simply say that it’s because of 2022 since its P/E appears to be the outlier in the data set. If we exclude it, the average will be 15.99x, which will reduce the TP to $131.92. Either way, TGT will still be cheaper. Also, its P/E today is the cheapest since 2020. The same applies to P/S Ratio, which gives a TP of $174.26.

However, this may not automatically lead to an uptrend. After all, the ratios only compare the cheapness or expensiveness of TGT relative to previous years. Also, it does not reveal its intrinsic value. The thing is, the market typically cares more about the direction of the EPS and sales. As you can see, the EPS and sales per share TTM are lower than in 2023 and 2024. So it’s not surprising that the stock price trend follows the trend. If we base it here, the assumption is that the EPS and sales for FY 2025 should increase, so the stock price will increase.

If we base it on my financial projection for February 2026, TGT may still take some time to rebound. Considering its performance last Q3, I believe that my financial projection in my previous coverage is still valid and conservative. Based on the improving YoY revenue trend in the past two quarters, I set my revenue decrease at -1%. With that, my estimated revenue high is $105.52B. For my revenue low, I simply set a revenue decrease of -5% to derive $101.24B. Meanwhile, I estimated COGS (72%) and operating expenses (23%) as a percentage of revenue. Lastly, I estimated depreciation and capex using the straight-line method. With that, my NOPAT and EBITDA are still lower than in February 2025. Hence, the stock price may stay within that range.

Technicals and Valuation, So What?

At this price, I say that it’s already a bit too late and also a bit too early to sell. As an investor here, it’s tricky to decide whether to buy or sell TGT. But sometimes the best thing to do is not do anything at all. If you’re my reader who followed my advice to buy at $89-91, I don’t advise you to add to your position. Lightening your shares can be logical, but I also won’t advise you to give it up completely. The downside risk is still tolerable if you ask me. Also, we cannot yet conclude that the recent price dip signals a reversal as I explained earlier. Profit-takers are still on the move at this point. Again, the uptrend still holds, and the price remains above the key support level. If it breaks below it, you may allot about 5-10% below $93.99 as your stop-loss price. So, that will range from $84.58 to $89.29.
