Another consideration is its ample dividends. At $4.56, TGT boasts a yield of 4.38%. In addition, the payout ratio remains decent at 59.68%. This means that only 60% of its earnings are used to pay dividends. The remaining 40% are part of retained earnings. This can be used to repay maturing borrowings, repurchase shares, and acquire fixed assets. The thing is, dividends are still sustainable, so I’d like to take advantage of it for now.

Risks

TGT still relies heavily on its physical stores, so its business model is still mainly brick and mortar. This makes it sensitive to inflation and cost pressures, which can affect its pricing flexibility. Today, inflation is 2.7%, which is still close to the 14-month high of 3.0%. This means that prices are still increasing, so purchasing power and operating costs may still be affected.

Competition is still a big challenge. It must watch out for Walmart (WMT) as another brick-and-mortar store with lower and sometimes predatory pricing. This can take away a portion of TGT’s demand. Also, Macy’s (M) and Nordstrom may become its direct competitors. We know that TGT sets its prices below these two premium retailers but higher than WMT and many dollar shops. However, inflation may distort pricing differentiation, especially if sales prices can no longer withstand cost pressures.

How About Amazon? It’s Not What You Think

I know that many investors are very wary of Amazon (AMZN). Even I watch out for its predatory tendencies. AMZN may squeeze TGT in convenience moat and logistics. Ad dollars may also be a factor if brands spend more on AMZN ads than retail promotions from TGT. This can affect its margin leverage.

However, the idea of Amazon killing Target is not simple and correct. There are pressures and overlapping factors, but this is not an existential knockout. After all, TGT is still defensively strong in terms of physical stores as an advantage. AMZN mainly exists in the online space, although it has some physical stores. With thousands of physical stores, TGT capitalizes on same-day pickup, easy returns, and drive-up. This is an aspect that AMZN cannot completely replicate yet. So, I think it’s WMT that TGT should watch out for.

Risk Mitigants and Core Strengths

Aside from its defensive plays against AMZN, I believe TGT can still hold its ground against WMT. Target has its own private labels, like Cat & Jack and Good & Gather. This can help TGT ensure brand loyalty, which can also be its edge over WMT. If you ask me, these brands still feel exclusive for Target. This is something that WMT cannot fully crack. Also, TGT still works and wins on apparel, home decor, and aesthetics. Impulsive buying, or what we often say, “I didn’t plan to buy this, but I did,” works more for TGT. After all, most of its products target affluent and brand-sensitive customers.

Considering all this, it’s not surprising that TGT maintains a decent performance despite inflationary headwinds. Its net sales amounted to $25.27B, down by 1.5% YoY from $25.66B. But this was better than in H1 2025, with a six-month YoY revenue decrease of 1.9%. Its services remained the core strength, especially advertising. F&B and hardlines also helped partially offset the weakness in other merchandise. This shows that its essential goods still have a strong demand.

In addition, TGT remains efficient considering the decrease in COGS. This is quite impressive amid stubborn inflation and an increased number of stores. In fact, its percentage of the combined amount of COGS and SG&A decreased to 76.6% versus 77.1%. This means that TGT was able to reduce its cost sensitivity. I think this can be rooted in two factors. First is the increased portion of digital sales versus in-store sales. Second, TGT is increasing its concentration on mid-sized stores rather than superstores. While this was enough to offset the decrease in sales, it still helped TGT to remain profitable as net income remained high at $689M.

Another core strength of TGT is its liquidity. Its operating cash flow of $1.127B last Q3 was enough to cover capex, borrowing repayments, and share repurchases. TGT complements it with prudent borrowing management to ensure it has sufficient cash. Currently, its cash level remains high at $3.8B. This is still more than enough to cover the maturing borrowings within a year.

In addition, its debt maturity profile remains decent. Note over 90% of the total amount will not mature within a year. Its issuance of $1.5B this year has a maturity of at least 10 years. The remaining $500M has a maturity of two years. Its maturity profile is still in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of 1.5x, which means that TGT only needs 1.5x years to repay the whole amount even if it doesn’t have to. If we only use 70% of EBITDA, which is $5.65B ($8.07B x 70%), the ratio will still be decent at 2.25x. This proves that TGT still has enough capital buffer to sustain its increasing operating capacity and dividends.

Lastly, TGT may benefit from the recent policy easing cycle. Lower interest rates can reduce the repricing of TGT’s variable-rate borrowings, which can improve its liquidity. Also, this can make credit cards more affordable and accessible, which can support discretionary spending. Moreover, it can boost economic activity, which can also increase employment and wages to bolster consumption.

Conclusion

Target Corporation is not a perfect investment considering the macroeconomic risks and intense competition it experiences. But its operational efficiencies, robust fundamentals, strategic pricing, and strong branding power allow it to get through these challenges. Its valuation is not exactly cheap but still reasonable with sustainable dividends. Technicals show some weakness but do not indicate a bearish reversal yet. All things considered, TGT is a hold for now.
